[L s=21.2 r=1.42 x=197 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Translator’s Preface
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=163 b=0 i=0 f=f3] A few words about Dostoevsky himself may help the English reader to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=181 b=0 i=0 f=f3] understand his work.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=199 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Dostoevsky was the son of a doctor. His parents were very hard-
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=217 b=0 i=0 f=f3] working and deeply religious people, but so poor that they lived with their
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=235 b=0 i=0 f=f3] five children in only two rooms. The father and mother spent their evenings
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=253 b=0 i=0 f=f3] in reading aloud to their children, generally from books of a serious
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=271 b=0 i=0 f=f3] character.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=289 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Though always sickly and delicate Dostoevsky came out third in the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=307 b=0 i=0 f=f3] final examination of the Petersburg school of Engineering. There he had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=325 b=0 i=0 f=f3] already begun his first work, “Poor Folk.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=343 b=0 i=0 f=f3] This story was published by the poet Nekrassov in his review and was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=361 b=0 i=0 f=f3] received with acclamations. The shy, unknown youth found himself
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=379 b=0 i=0 f=f3] instantly something of a celebrity. A brilliant and successful career seemed
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=397 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to open before him, but those hopes were soon dashed. In 1849 he was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=415 b=0 i=0 f=f3] arrested.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=433 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Though neither by temperament nor conviction a revolutionist,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=451 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Dostoevsky was one of a little group of young men who met together to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=469 b=0 i=0 f=f3] read Fourier and Proudhon. He was accused of “taking part in conversations
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=487 b=0 i=0 f=f3] against the censorship, of reading a letter from Byelinsky to Gogol, and of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=505 b=0 i=0 f=f3] knowing of the intention to set up a printing press.” Under Nicholas I. (that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=523 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “stern and just man,” as Maurice Baring calls him) this was enough, and he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=541 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was condemned to death. After eight months’ imprisonment he was with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=559 b=0 i=0 f=f3] twenty-one others taken out to the Semyonovsky Square to be shot. Writing
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=577 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to his brother Mihail, Dostoevsky says: “They snapped words over our
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=595 b=0 i=0 f=f3] heads, and they made us put on the white shirts worn by persons
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=613 b=0 i=0 f=f3] condemned to death. Thereupon we were bound in threes to stakes, to suffer
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=631 b=0 i=0 f=f3] execution. Being the third in the row, I concluded I had only a few minutes
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=649 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of life before me. I thought of you and your dear ones and I contrived to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=667 b=0 i=0 f=f3] kiss Plestcheiev and Dourov, who were next to me, and to bid them
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=685 b=0 i=0 f=f3] farewell. Suddenly the troops beat a tattoo, we were unbound, brought back

[PAGE 8]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] upon the scaffold, and informed that his Majesty had spared us our lives.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The sentence was commuted to hard labour.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] One of the prisoners, Grigoryev, went mad as soon as he was untied,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and never regained his sanity.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The intense suffering of this experience left a lasting stamp on
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Dostoevsky’s mind. Though his religious temper led him in the end to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] accept every suffering with resignation and to regard it as a blessing in his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] own case, he constantly recurs to the subject in his writings. He describes
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the awful agony of the condemned man and insists on the cruelty of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] inflicting such torture. Then followed four years of penal servitude, spent in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the company of common criminals in Siberia, where he began the “Dead
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] House,” and some years of service in a disciplinary battalion.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He had shown signs of some obscure nervous disease before his arrest
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and this now developed into violent attacks of epilepsy, from which he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] suffered for the rest of his life. The fits occurred three or four times a year
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and were more frequent in periods of great strain. In 1859 he was allowed
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to return to Russia. He started a journal—“Vremya,” which was forbidden
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] by the Censorship through a misunderstanding. In 1864 he lost his first wife
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and his brother Mihail. He was in terrible poverty, yet he took upon himself
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the payment of his brother’s debts. He started another journal—“The
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Epoch,” which within a few months was also prohibited. He was weighed
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] down by debt, his brother’s family was dependent on him, he was forced to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] write at heart-breaking speed, and is said never to have corrected his work.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The later years of his life were much softened by the tenderness and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] devotion of his second wife.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] In June 1880 he made his famous speech at the unveiling of the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] monument to Pushkin in Moscow and he was received with extraordinary
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] demonstrations of love and honour.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] A few months later Dostoevsky died. He was followed to the grave by a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] vast multitude of mourners, who “gave the hapless man the funeral of a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] king.” He is still probably the most widely read writer in Russia.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] In the words of a Russian critic, who seeks to explain the feeling
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] inspired by Dostoevsky: “He was one of ourselves, a man of our blood and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] our bone, but one who has suffered and has seen so much more deeply than
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] we have his insight impresses us as wisdom… that wisdom of the heart
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] which we seek that we may learn from it how to live. All his other gifts

[PAGE 9]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] came to him from nature, this he won for himself and through it he became
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] great.”

[PAGE 10]
[L s=27.5 r=1.00 x=267 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Part I

[PAGE 11]
[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=223 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter One
[L s=27.5 r=1.83 x=77 y=170 b=0 i=0 f=f3] O
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=297 y=179 b=0 i=0 f=f3] early in July a young man came out of
[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=97 y=183 b=0 i=0 f=f3] N AN EXCEPTIONALLY HOT EVENING
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the garret in which he lodged in S. Place and walked slowly, as though in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=219 b=0 i=0 f=f3] hesitation, towards K. bridge.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He had successfully avoided meeting his landlady on the staircase. His
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=255 b=0 i=0 f=f3] garret was under the roof of a high, five-storied house and was more like a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] cupboard than a room. The landlady who provided him with garret, dinners,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and attendance, lived on the floor below, and every time he went out he was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] obliged to pass her kitchen, the door of which invariably stood open. And
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f3] each time he passed, the young man had a sick, frightened feeling, which
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] made him scowl and feel ashamed. He was hopelessly in debt to his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=363 b=0 i=0 f=f3] landlady, and was afraid of meeting her.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=381 b=0 i=0 f=f3] This was not because he was cowardly and abject, quite the contrary;
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=399 b=0 i=0 f=f3] but for some time past he had been in an overstrained irritable condition,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=417 b=0 i=0 f=f3] verging on hypochondria. He had become so completely absorbed in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=435 b=0 i=0 f=f3] himself, and isolated from his fellows that he dreaded meeting, not only his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=453 b=0 i=0 f=f3] landlady, but anyone at all. He was crushed by poverty, but the anxieties of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=471 b=0 i=0 f=f3] his position had of late ceased to weigh upon him. He had given up
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=489 b=0 i=0 f=f3] attending to matters of practical importance; he had lost all desire to do so.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=507 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Nothing that any landlady could do had a real terror for him. But to be
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=525 b=0 i=0 f=f3] stopped on the stairs, to be forced to listen to her trivial, irrelevant gossip, to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=543 b=0 i=0 f=f3] pestering demands for payment, threats and complaints, and to rack his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=561 b=0 i=0 f=f3] brains for excuses, to prevaricate, to lie—no, rather than that, he would
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=579 b=0 i=0 f=f3] creep down the stairs like a cat and slip out unseen.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=597 b=0 i=0 f=f3] This evening, however, on coming out into the street, he became acutely
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=615 b=0 i=0 f=f3] aware of his fears.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=633 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I want to attempt a thing like that and am frightened by these trifles,”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=651 b=0 i=0 f=f3] he thought, with an odd smile. “Hm… yes, all is in a man’s hands and he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=669 b=0 i=0 f=f3] lets it all slip from cowardice, that’s an axiom. It would be interesting to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=687 b=0 i=0 f=f3] know what it is men are most afraid of. Taking a new step, uttering a new

[PAGE 12]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] word is what they fear most…. But I am talking too much. It’s because I
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] chatter that I do nothing. Or perhaps it is that I chatter because I do nothing.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] I’ve learned to chatter this last month, lying for days together in my den
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] thinking… of Jack the Giant-killer. Why am I going there now? Am I
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] capable of that? Is that serious? It is not serious at all. It’s simply a fantasy
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to amuse myself; a plaything! Yes, maybe it is a plaything.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The heat in the street was terrible: and the airlessness, the bustle and the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] plaster, scaffolding, bricks, and dust all about him, and that special
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Petersburg stench, so familiar to all who are unable to get out of town in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] summer—all worked painfully upon the young man’s already overwrought
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] nerves. The insufferable stench from the pot-houses, which are particularly
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] numerous in that part of the town, and the drunken men whom he met
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] continually, although it was a working day, completed the revolting misery
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of the picture. An expression of the profoundest disgust gleamed for a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] moment in the young man’s refined face. He was, by the way, exceptionally
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] handsome, above the average in height, slim, well-built, with beautiful dark
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] eyes and dark brown hair. Soon he sank into deep thought, or more
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] accurately speaking into a complete blankness of mind; he walked along not
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] observing what was about him and not caring to observe it. From time to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] time, he would mutter something, from the habit of talking to himself, to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] which he had just confessed. At these moments he would become conscious
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that his ideas were sometimes in a tangle and that he was very weak; for
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] two days he had scarcely tasted food.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He was so badly dressed that even a man accustomed to shabbiness
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] would have been ashamed to be seen in the street in such rags. In that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] quarter of the town, however, scarcely any shortcoming in dress would have
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] created surprise. Owing to the proximity of the Hay Market, the number of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] establishments of bad character, the preponderance of the trading and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] working class population crowded in these streets and alleys in the heart of

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] however queer, would have caused surprise. But there was such

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] yet when a drunken man who, for some unknown reason, was being taken

[PAGE 13]

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] but completely worn out, rusty with age, all torn and bespattered, brimless
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and bent on one side in a most unseemly fashion. Not shame, however, but
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] quite another feeling akin to terror had overtaken him.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I knew it,” he muttered in confusion, “I thought so! That’s the worst of

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He had not far to go; he knew indeed how many steps it was from the

[PAGE 18]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “All that’s nonsense,” he said hopefully, “and there is nothing in it all to

[PAGE 19]
[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=221 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter Two

[PAGE 35]

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] there are no barriers and it’s all as it should be.”

[PAGE 38]

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] flowers especially caught his attention; he gazed at them longer than at
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] anything. He was met, too, by luxurious carriages and by men and women
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] on horseback; he watched them with curious eyes and forgot about them
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] before they had vanished from his sight. Once he stood still and counted his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] money; he found he had thirty copecks. “Twenty to the policeman, three to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Nastasya for the letter, so I must have given forty-seven or fifty to the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Marmeladovs yesterday,” he thought, reckoning it up for some unknown
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] reason, but he soon forgot with what object he had taken the money out of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] his pocket. He recalled it on passing an eating-house or tavern, and felt that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] he was hungry…. Going into the tavern he drank a glass of vodka and ate a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] pie of some sort. He finished eating it as he walked away. It was a long
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] while since he had taken vodka and it had an effect upon him at once,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] though he only drank a wineglassful. His legs felt suddenly heavy and a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] great drowsiness came upon him. He turned homewards, but reaching
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Petrovsky Ostrov he stopped completely exhausted, turned off the road into
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the bushes, sank down upon the grass and instantly fell asleep.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] In a morbid condition of the brain, dreams often have a singular
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] actuality, vividness, and extraordinary semblance of reality. At times
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] monstrous images are created, but the setting and the whole picture are so
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] truth-like and filled with details so delicate, so unexpectedly, but so
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] artistically consistent, that the dreamer, were he an artist like Pushkin or
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Turgenev even, could never have invented them in the waking state. Such

[PAGE 64]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] sick dreams always remain long in the memory and make a powerful
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] impression on the overwrought and deranged nervous system.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov had a fearful dream. He dreamt he was back in his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] childhood in the little town of his birth. He was a child about seven years
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] old, walking into the country with his father on the evening of a holiday. It
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was a grey and heavy day, the country was exactly as he remembered it;
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] indeed he recalled it far more vividly in his dream than he had done in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] memory. The little town stood on a level flat as bare as the hand, not even a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] willow near it; only in the far distance, a copse lay, a dark blur on the very
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] edge of the horizon. A few paces beyond the last market garden stood a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] tavern, a big tavern, which had always aroused in him a feeling of aversion,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] even of fear, when he walked by it with his father. There was always a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] crowd there, always shouting, laughter and abuse, hideous hoarse singing
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and often fighting. Drunken and horrible-looking figures were hanging
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] about the tavern. He used to cling close to his father, trembling all over
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] when he met them. Near the tavern the road became a dusty track, the dust
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of which was always black. It was a winding road, and about a hundred
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] paces further on, it turned to the right to the graveyard. In the middle of the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] graveyard stood a stone church with a green cupola where he used to go to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] mass two or three times a year with his father and mother, when a service
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was held in memory of his grandmother, who had long been dead, and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] whom he had never seen. On these occasions they used to take on a white
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] dish tied up in a table napkin a special sort of rice pudding with raisins
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] stuck in it in the shape of a cross. He loved that church, the old-fashioned,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] unadorned ikons and the old priest with the shaking head. Near his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] grandmother’s grave, which was marked by a stone, was the little grave of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] his younger brother who had died at six months old. He did not remember
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] him at all, but he had been told about his little brother, and whenever he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] visited the graveyard he used religiously and reverently to cross himself and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to bow down and kiss the little grave. And now he dreamt that he was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] walking with his father past the tavern on the way to the graveyard; he was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] holding his father’s hand and looking with dread at the tavern. A peculiar
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] circumstance attracted his attention: there seemed to be some kind of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] festivity going on, there were crowds of gaily dressed townspeople, peasant

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] less drunk. Near the entrance of the tavern stood a cart, but a strange cart. It

[PAGE 65]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was one of those big carts usually drawn by heavy cart-horses and laden
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] with casks of wine or other heavy goods. He always liked looking at those
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] great cart-horses, with their long manes, thick legs, and slow even pace,

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] nags which he had often seen straining their utmost under a heavy load of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] wood or hay, especially when the wheels were stuck in the mud or in a rut.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] And the peasants would beat them so cruelly, sometimes even about the

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] wearing red and blue shirts and coats thrown over their shoulders.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] crowbar. The mare stretched out her head, drew a long breath and died.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Why wouldn’t she gallop then?”

[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=233 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter Six

[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=94 y=183 b=0 i=0 f=f3] ATER ON ASKOLNIKOV HAPPENED
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] had invited Lizaveta. It was a very ordinary matter and there was nothing

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] looking for a dealer. This was Lizaveta’s business. She undertook such jobs
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and was frequently employed, as she was very honest and always fixed a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] fair price and stuck to it. She spoke as a rule little and, as we have said

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] But Raskolnikov had become superstitious of late. The traces of

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] dealings with her. But she is an awful old harpy….”

[PAGE 74]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “But you say she is hideous?” observed the officer.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Oh, well, brother, but we have to correct and direct nature, and, but for

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] had also picked up at the same time in the street. Putting the iron which was

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] crossing and re-crossing the thread round them; then wrapped them
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] carefully and daintily in clean white paper and tied up the parcel so that it
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] would be very difficult to untie it. This was in order to divert the attention
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of the old woman for a time, while she was trying to undo the knot, and so
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to gain a moment. The iron strip was added to give weight, so that the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] woman might not guess the first minute that the “thing” was made of wood.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] All this had been stored by him beforehand under the sofa. He had only just
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] got the pledge out when he heard someone suddenly about in the yard.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “It struck six long ago.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Long ago! My God!”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He rushed to the door, listened, caught up his hat and began to descend
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] his thirteen steps cautiously, noiselessly, like a cat. He had still the most
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] important thing to do—to steal the axe from the kitchen. That the deed must
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] be done with an axe he had decided long ago. He had also a pocket pruning-

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and so resolved finally on the axe. We may note in passing, one peculiarity

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] And, indeed, if it had ever happened that everything to the least point

[PAGE 79]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] house, especially in the evenings; she would run in to the neighbours or to a

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] But those were all trifles which he had not even begun to consider, and

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] At first—long before indeed—he had been much occupied with one
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] question; why almost all crimes are so badly concealed and so easily

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] reached its highest point just before the perpetration of the crime, continued

[PAGE 80]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] with equal violence at the moment of the crime and for longer or shorter
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] time after, according to the individual case, and then passed off like any

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] something of the nature of disease, he did not yet feel able to decide.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] When he reached these conclusions, he decided that in his own case

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] revolting. “And what a chance I have lost for ever!” he muttered, standing

[PAGE 83]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] But his heart did not leave off. On the contrary, as though to spite him, it

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] No answer. To go on ringing was useless and out of place. The old

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] some knowledge of her habits… and once more he put his ear to the door.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Either his senses were peculiarly keen (which it is difficult to suppose), or

[PAGE 89]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] But a sort of blankness, even dreaminess, had begun by degrees to take

[PAGE 90]

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Hey there! Catch the brute!”

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Mitka! Mitka! Mitka! Mitka! Mitka! Blast him!”

[PAGE 94]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] up to the fourth floor, talking loudly. He waited, went out on tiptoe and ran

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of reflection that he walked straight to the door and opened it. If the porter

[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=223 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter One

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=258 y=179 b=0 i=0 f=f3] . Now and then he seemed to wake up, and at
[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=92 y=183 b=0 i=0 f=f3] O HE LAY A VERY LONG WHILE
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] such moments he noticed that it was far into the night, but it did not occur
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=219 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to him to get up. At last he noticed that it was beginning to get light. He was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f3] lying on his back, still dazed from his recent oblivion. Fearful, despairing
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=255 b=0 i=0 f=f3] cries rose shrilly from the street, sounds which he heard every night, indeed,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] under his window after two o’clock. They woke him up now.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Ah! the drunken men are coming out of the taverns,” he thought, “it’s
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] past two o’clock,” and at once he leaped up, as though someone had pulled
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f3] him from the sofa.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “What! Past two o’clock!”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=363 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He sat down on the sofa—and instantly recollected everything! All at

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=471 b=0 i=0 f=f3] began listening—everything in the house was asleep. With amazement he

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=507 b=0 i=0 f=f3] could have come in the night before without fastening the door, and have
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=525 b=0 i=0 f=f3] flung himself on the sofa without undressing, without even taking his hat
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=543 b=0 i=0 f=f3] off. It had fallen off and was lying on the floor near his pillow.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=561 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “If anyone had come in, what would he have thought? That I’m drunk

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=687 b=0 i=0 f=f3] search three times.

[PAGE 98]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] But there seemed to be nothing, no trace, except in one place, where

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] then of taking them out and hiding them! He had not even thought of them

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the paper: “They’re in! All out of sight, and the purse too!” he thought

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] money, and so had not prepared a hiding-place.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Then a strange idea entered his head; that, perhaps, all his clothes were

[PAGE 115]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] sides; it would look suspicious for a man to go down on purpose, stop, and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] throw something into the water. And what if the boxes were to float instead
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of sinking? And of course they would. Even as it was, everyone he met

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] him. “Why is it, or can it be my fancy?” he thought.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] At last the thought struck him that it might be better to go to the Neva.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] There were not so many people there, he would be less observed, and it
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] would be more convenient in every way, above all it was further off. He
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] wondered how he could have been wandering for a good half-hour, worried
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and anxious in this dangerous past without thinking of it before. And that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] half-hour he had lost over an irrational plan, simply because he had thought

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] aware of it. He certainly must make haste.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He walked towards the Neva along V-Prospect, but on the way another

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the left. Here was a deserted fenced-off place where rubbish of different
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] sorts was lying. At the end of the court, the corner of a low, smutty, stone

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] throw it, he thought. Not seeing anyone in the yard, he slipped in, and at

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] about his going in. “Here I could throw it all in a heap and get away!”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Looking round once more, with his hand already in his pocket, he

[PAGE 116]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] unhewn stone, weighing perhaps sixty pounds. The other side of the wall

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He bent down over the stone, seized the top of it firmly in both hands,

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] in the ground, and he immediately emptied his pocket into it. The purse lay

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] this shabby and impudent person had introduced himself as a student.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] When he had dressed in entirely new clothes, he looked at the money lying

[PAGE 159]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] From old habit he took his usual walk in the direction of the Hay
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Market. A dark-haired young man with a barrel organ was standing in the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] road in front of a little general shop and was grinding out a very sentimental
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] song. He was accompanying a girl of fifteen, who stood on the pavement in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] front of him. She was dressed up in a crinoline, a mantle and a straw hat
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] with a flame-coloured feather in it, all very old and shabby. In a strong and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] rather agreeable voice, cracked and coarsened by street singing, she sang in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] hope of getting a copper from the shop. Raskolnikov joined two or three
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] listeners, took out a five copeck piece and put it in the girl’s hand. She
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] broke off abruptly on a sentimental high note, shouted sharply to the organ
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] grinder “Come on,” and both moved on to the next shop.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Do you like street music?” said Raskolnikov, addressing a middle-aged
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] man standing idly by him. The man looked at him, startled and wondering.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Zametov, left alone, sat for a long time in the same place, plunged in

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] certain point and had made up his mind for him conclusively.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] strange. He walked to the window and sat down on the window-sill. There
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] were two workmen, both young fellows, but one much younger than the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] other. They were papering the walls with a new white paper covered with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] lilac flowers, instead of the old, dirty, yellow one. Raskolnikov for some
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] reason felt horribly annoyed by this. He looked at the new paper with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] dislike, as though he felt sorry to have it all so changed. The workmen had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] obviously stayed beyond their time and now they were hurriedly rolling up
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] their paper and getting ready to go home. They took no notice of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov’s coming in; they were talking. Raskolnikov folded his arms
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and listened.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “She comes to me in the morning,” said the elder to the younger, “very
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] early, all dressed up. ‘Why are you preening and prinking?’ says I. ‘I am
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] ready to do anything to please you, Tit Vassilitch!’ That’s a way of going
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] on! And she dressed up like a regular fashion book!”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “And what is a fashion book?” the younger one asked. He obviously
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] regarded the other as an authority.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “A fashion book is a lot of pictures, coloured, and they come to the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] tailors here every Saturday, by post from abroad, to show folks how to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] dress, the male sex as well as the female. They’re pictures. The gentlemen
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] are generally wearing fur coats and for the ladies’ fluffles, they’re beyond
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] anything you can fancy.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “There’s nothing you can’t find in Petersburg,” the younger cried
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] enthusiastically, “except father and mother, there’s everything!”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Except them, there’s everything to be found, my boy,” the elder
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] declared sententiously.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov got up and walked into the other room where the strong
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] box, the bed, and the chest of drawers had been; the room seemed to him

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “What do you want?” he asked suddenly.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Instead of answering Raskolnikov went into the passage and pulled the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] bell. The same bell, the same cracked note. He rang it a second and a third

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Well, what do you want? Who are you?” the workman shouted, going

[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=211 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter Seven

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] at a loss and kept repeating:
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “What a misfortune! Good Lord, what a misfortune!”

[PAGE 179]

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] But the thing was straightforward and legitimate, and in any case help was

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] round! I’ll pay, I’ll make it worth your while,” he muttered.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Katerina Ivanovna had just begun, as she always did at every free

[PAGE 181]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] What’s this?” she cried, noticing a crowd in the passage and the men, who

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] child’s bare feet.

[PAGE 185]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “He will die immediately.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Is there really no hope?”

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “If you like…. But I warn you it will be perfectly useless.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] At that moment other steps were heard; the crowd in the passage parted,

[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=221 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter Two
[L s=27.5 r=1.83 x=77 y=170 b=0 i=0 f=f3] R
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=303 y=179 b=0 i=0 f=f3] at eight o’clock, troubled and serious.
[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=95 y=183 b=0 i=0 f=f3] AZUMIHIN WAKED UP NEXT MORNING
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He found himself confronted with many new and unlooked-for perplexities.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=219 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He had never expected that he would ever wake up feeling like that. He
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f3] remembered every detail of the previous day and he knew that a perfectly
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=255 b=0 i=0 f=f3] novel experience had befallen him, that he had received an impression
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] unlike anything he had known before. At the same time he recognised
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] clearly that the dream which had fired his imagination was hopelessly
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] unattainable—so unattainable that he felt positively ashamed of it, and he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f3] hastened to pass to the other more practical cares and difficulties
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] bequeathed him by that “thrice accursed yesterday.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=363 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The most awful recollection of the previous day was the way he had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=381 b=0 i=0 f=f3] shown himself “base and mean,” not only because he had been drunk, but
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=399 b=0 i=0 f=f3] because he had taken advantage of the young girl’s position to abuse her
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=417 b=0 i=0 f=f3] fiancé in his stupid jealousy, knowing nothing of their mutual relations and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=435 b=0 i=0 f=f3] obligations and next to nothing of the man himself. And what right had he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=453 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to criticise him in that hasty and unguarded manner? Who had asked for his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=471 b=0 i=0 f=f3] opinion? Was it thinkable that such a creature as Avdotya Romanovna
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=489 b=0 i=0 f=f3] would be marrying an unworthy man for money? So there must be
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=507 b=0 i=0 f=f3] something in him. The lodgings? But after all how could he know the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=525 b=0 i=0 f=f3] character of the lodgings? He was furnishing a flat… Foo! how despicable
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=543 b=0 i=0 f=f3] it all was! And what justification was it that he was drunk? Such a stupid
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=561 b=0 i=0 f=f3] excuse was even more degrading! In wine is truth, and the truth had all
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=579 b=0 i=0 f=f3] come out, “that is, all the uncleanness of his coarse and envious heart”! And
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=597 b=0 i=0 f=f3] would such a dream ever be permissible to him, Razumihin? What was he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=615 b=0 i=0 f=f3] beside such a girl—he, the drunken noisy braggart of last night? Was it
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=633 b=0 i=0 f=f3] possible to imagine so absurd and cynical a juxtaposition? Razumihin
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=651 b=0 i=0 f=f3] blushed desperately at the very idea and suddenly the recollection forced

[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=213 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter Three

[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=109 y=183 b=0 i=0 f=f3] E IS WELL QUITE WELL
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He had come in ten minutes earlier and was sitting in the same place as

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] His brows were knitted, his lips compressed, his eyes feverish. He spoke

[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=223 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter Five
[L s=27.5 r=1.83 x=77 y=170 b=0 i=0 f=f3] R
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=377 y=179 b=0 i=0 f=f3] . He came in looking as
[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=95 y=183 b=0 i=0 f=f3] ASKOLNIKOV WAS ALREADY ENTERING THE ROOM
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] though he had the utmost difficulty not to burst out laughing again. Behind
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=219 b=0 i=0 f=f3] him Razumihin strode in gawky and awkward, shamefaced and red as a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f3] peony, with an utterly crestfallen and ferocious expression. His face and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=255 b=0 i=0 f=f3] whole figure really were ridiculous at that moment and amply justified
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov’s laughter. Raskolnikov, not waiting for an introduction,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] bowed to Porfiry Petrovitch, who stood in the middle of the room looking
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] inquiringly at them. He held out his hand and shook hands, still apparently
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f3] making desperate efforts to subdue his mirth and utter a few words to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] introduce himself. But he had no sooner succeeded in assuming a serious air
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=363 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and muttering something when he suddenly glanced again as though
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=381 b=0 i=0 f=f3] accidentally at Razumihin, and could no longer control himself: his stifled
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=399 b=0 i=0 f=f3] laughter broke out the more irresistibly the more he tried to restrain it. The

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=453 b=0 i=0 f=f3] naturalness. Razumihin strengthened this impression as though on purpose.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=471 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Fool! You fiend,” he roared, waving his arm which at once struck a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=489 b=0 i=0 f=f3] little round table with an empty tea-glass on it. Everything was sent flying
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=507 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and crashing.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=525 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “But why break chairs, gentlemen? You know it’s a loss to the Crown,”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=543 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Porfiry Petrovitch quoted gaily.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=561 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov was still laughing, with his hand in Porfiry Petrovitch’s,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=579 b=0 i=0 f=f3] but anxious not to overdo it, awaited the right moment to put a natural end
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=597 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to it. Razumihin, completely put to confusion by upsetting the table and

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=633 b=0 i=0 f=f3] sharply to the window where he stood looking out with his back to the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=651 b=0 i=0 f=f3] company with a fiercely scowling countenance, seeing nothing. Porfiry
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=669 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Petrovitch laughed and was ready to go on laughing, but obviously looked
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=687 b=0 i=0 f=f3] for explanations. Zametov had been sitting in the corner, but he rose at the

[PAGE 248]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] visitors’ entrance and was standing in expectation with a smile on his lips,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] though he looked with surprise and even it seemed incredulity at the whole

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] unexpected presence struck Raskolnikov unpleasantly.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I’ve got to think of that,” he thought. “Excuse me, please,” he began,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] affecting extreme embarrassment. “Raskolnikov.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Not at all, very pleasant to see you… and how pleasantly you’ve come
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] in…. Why, won’t he even say good-morning?” Porfiry Petrovitch nodded at

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Upon my honour I don’t know why he is in such a rage with me. I only
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] told him as we came along that he was like Romeo… and proved it. And
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that was all, I think!”

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Porfiry Petrovitch was wearing a dressing-gown, very clean linen, and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] trodden-down slippers. He was a man of about five and thirty, short, stout

[PAGE 249]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] white, blinking eyelashes. The expression of those eyes was strangely out of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] keeping with his somewhat womanish figure, and gave it something far

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I was awfully sick of them yesterday.” Raskolnikov addressed Porfiry
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] suddenly with a smile of insolent defiance, “I ran away from them to take
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] lodgings where they wouldn’t find me, and took a lot of money with me.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Mr. Zametov there saw it. I say, Mr. Zametov, was I sensible or delirious
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] yesterday; settle our dispute.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He could have strangled Zametov at that moment, so hateful were his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] expression and his silence to him.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “In my opinion you talked sensibly and even artfully, but you were
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] extremely irritable,” Zametov pronounced dryly.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “And Nikodim Fomitch was telling me to-day,” put in Porfiry
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Petrovitch, “that he met you very late last night in the lodging of a man who
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] had been run over.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “And there,” said Razumihin, “weren’t you mad then? You gave your
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] last penny to the widow for the funeral. If you wanted to help, give fifteen

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] away all the twenty-five at once!”

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “But you might give us some tea! My throat’s dry,” cried Razumihin.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Capital idea! Perhaps we will all keep you company. Wouldn’t you

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov’s thoughts were in a whirl. He was in terrible exasperation.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “The worst of it is they don’t disguise it; they don’t care to stand on

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] it! I shall get up and throw the whole truth in your ugly faces, and you’ll see

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] which I regret to say you merely suggested without working it out clearly.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] There is, if you recollect, a suggestion that there are certain persons who
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] can… that is, not precisely are able to, but have a perfect right to commit
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] breaches of morality and crimes, and that the law is not for them.”

[PAGE 257]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov smiled at the exaggerated and intentional distortion of his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] idea.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “What? What do you mean? A right to crime? But not because of the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] influence of environment?” Razumihin inquired with some alarm even.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “No, not exactly because of it,” answered Porfiry. “In his article all men
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] are divided into ‘ordinary’ and ‘extraordinary.’ Ordinary men have to live in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] submission, have no right to transgress the law, because, don’t you see, they
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] are ordinary. But extraordinary men have a right to commit any crime and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to transgress the law in any way, just because they are extraordinary. That
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was your idea, if I am not mistaken?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “What do you mean? That can’t be right?” Razumihin muttered in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] bewilderment.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov smiled again. He saw the point at once, and knew where
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] they wanted to drive him. He decided to take up the challenge.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “That wasn’t quite my contention,” he began simply and modestly. “Yet
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] I admit that you have stated it almost correctly; perhaps, if you like,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] perfectly so.” (It almost gave him pleasure to admit this.) “The only
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] difference is that I don’t contend that extraordinary people are always
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] bound to commit breaches of morals, as you call it. In fact, I doubt whether
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] such an argument could be published. I simply hinted that an
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] ‘extraordinary’ man has the right… that is not an official right, but an inner
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] right to decide in his own conscience to overstep… certain obstacles, and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] only in case it is essential for the practical fulfilment of his idea
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] (sometimes, perhaps, of benefit to the whole of humanity). You say that my
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] article isn’t definite; I am ready to make it as clear as I can. Perhaps I am
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] right in thinking you want me to; very well. I maintain that if the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] discoveries of Kepler and Newton could not have been made known except
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] by sacrificing the lives of one, a dozen, a hundred, or more men, Newton
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] would have had the right, would indeed have been in duty bound… to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] eliminate the dozen or the hundred men for the sake of making his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] discoveries known to the whole of humanity. But it does not follow from
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that that Newton had a right to murder people right and left and to steal
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] every day in the market. Then, I remember, I maintain in my article that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] all… well, legislators and leaders of men, such as Lycurgus, Solon,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Mahomet, Napoleon, and so on, were all without exception criminals, from
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the very fact that, making a new law, they transgressed the ancient one,

[PAGE 258]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] handed down from their ancestors and held sacred by the people, and they
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] did not stop short at bloodshed either, if that bloodshed—often of innocent
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] persons fighting bravely in defence of ancient law—were of use to their
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] cause. It’s remarkable, in fact, that the majority, indeed, of these benefactors
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and leaders of humanity were guilty of terrible carnage. In short, I maintain
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that all great men or even men a little out of the common, that is to say
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] capable of giving some new word, must from their very nature be criminals
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] —more or less, of course. Otherwise it’s hard for them to get out of the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] common rut; and to remain in the common rut is what they can’t submit to,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] from their very nature again, and to my mind they ought not, indeed, to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] submit to it. You see that there is nothing particularly new in all that. The
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] same thing has been printed and read a thousand times before. As for my
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] division of people into ordinary and extraordinary, I acknowledge that it’s
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] somewhat arbitrary, but I don’t insist upon exact numbers. I only believe in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] my leading idea that men are in general divided by a law of nature into two
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] categories, inferior (ordinary), that is, so to say, material that serves only to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] reproduce its kind, and men who have the gift or the talent to utter a new
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] word. There are, of course, innumerable sub-divisions, but the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] distinguishing features of both categories are fairly well marked. The first
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] category, generally speaking, are men conservative in temperament and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] law-abiding; they live under control and love to be controlled. To my
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] thinking it is their duty to be controlled, because that’s their vocation, and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] there is nothing humiliating in it for them. The second category all
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] transgress the law; they are destroyers or disposed to destruction according
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to their capacities. The crimes of these men are of course relative and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] varied; for the most part they seek in very varied ways the destruction of the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] present for the sake of the better. But if such a one is forced for the sake of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] his idea to step over a corpse or wade through blood, he can, I maintain,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] find within himself, in his conscience, a sanction for wading through blood
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] —that depends on the idea and its dimensions, note that. It’s only in that

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] with the legal question). There’s no need for such anxiety, however; the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] masses will scarcely ever admit this right, they punish them or hang them
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] (more or less), and in doing so fulfil quite justly their conservative vocation.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] But the same masses set these criminals on a pedestal in the next generation
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and worship them (more or less). The first category is always the man of the

[PAGE 259]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] present, the second the man of the future. The first preserve the world and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] people it, the second move the world and lead it to its goal. Each class has
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] an equal right to exist. In fact, all have equal rights with me—and vive la
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] guerre éternelle—till the New Jerusalem, of course!”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Then you believe in the New Jerusalem, do you?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I do,” Raskolnikov answered firmly; as he said these words and during
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the whole preceding tirade he kept his eyes on one spot on the carpet.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “And… and do you believe in God? Excuse my curiosity.”

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Literally.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “You don’t say so…. I asked from curiosity. Excuse me. But let us go

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] themselves into the ‘new movement,’ and this quite sincerely. Meanwhile

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] That law, of course, is unknown at present, but I am convinced that it exists,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and one day may become known. The vast mass of mankind is mere

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] independence one in a hundred thousand. The man of genius is one of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] millions, and the great geniuses, the crown of humanity, appear on earth
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] perhaps one in many thousand millions. In fact I have not peeped into the

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] definite law, it cannot be a matter of chance.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Why, are you both joking?” Razumihin cried at last. “There you sit,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] making fun of one another. Are you serious, Rodya?”

[PAGE 261]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov raised his pale and almost mournful face and made no
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] reply. And the unconcealed, persistent, nervous, and discourteous sarcasm
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of Porfiry seemed strange to Razumihin beside that quiet and mournful
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] face.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Well, brother, if you are really serious… You are right, of course, in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] saying that it’s not new, that it’s like what we’ve read and heard a thousand
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] times already; but what is really original in all this, and is exclusively your
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] own, to my horror, is that you sanction bloodshed in the name of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] conscience, and, excuse my saying so, with such fanaticism…. That, I take
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] it, is the point of your article. But that sanction of bloodshed by conscience
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] is to my mind… more terrible than the official, legal sanction of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] bloodshed….”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “You are quite right, it is more terrible,” Porfiry agreed.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Yes, you must have exaggerated! There is some mistake, I shall read it.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] You can’t think that! I shall read it.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “All that is not in the article, there’s only a hint of it,” said Raskolnikov.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Yes, yes.” Porfiry couldn’t sit still. “Your attitude to crime is pretty
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] clear to me now, but… excuse me for my impertinence (I am really
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] ashamed to be worrying you like this), you see, you’ve removed my anxiety
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] as to the two grades getting mixed, but… there are various practical
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] possibilities that make me uneasy! What if some man or youth imagines
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that he is a Lycurgus or Mahomet—a future one of course—and suppose he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] begins to remove all obstacles…. He has some great enterprise before him
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and needs money for it… and tries to get it… do you see?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Zametov gave a sudden guffaw in his corner. Raskolnikov did not even
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] raise his eyes to him.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I must admit,” he went on calmly, “that such cases certainly must arise.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The vain and foolish are particularly apt to fall into that snare; young people
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] especially.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Yes, you see. Well then?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “What then?” Raskolnikov smiled in reply; “that’s not my fault. So it is
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and so it always will be. He said just now (he nodded at Razumihin) that I
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] sanction bloodshed. Society is too well protected by prisons, banishment,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] criminal investigators, penal servitude. There’s no need to be uneasy. You
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] have but to catch the thief.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “And what if we do catch him?”

[PAGE 262]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Then he gets what he deserves.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “You are certainly logical. But what of his conscience?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Why do you care about that?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Simply from humanity.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “If he has a conscience he will suffer for his mistake. That will be his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] punishment—as well as the prison.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “But the real geniuses,” asked Razumihin frowning, “those who have
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the right to murder? Oughtn’t they to suffer at all even for the blood they’ve

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] will suffer if he is sorry for his victim. Pain and suffering are always
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] inevitable for a large intelligence and a deep heart. The really great men
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] must, I think, have great sadness on earth,” he added dreamily, not in the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] tone of the conversation.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He raised his eyes, looked earnestly at them all, smiled, and took his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] cap. He was too quiet by comparison with his manner at his entrance, and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] he felt this. Everyone got up.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Well, you may abuse me, be angry with me if you like,” Porfiry
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Petrovitch began again, “but I can’t resist. Allow me one little question (I
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] know I am troubling you). There is just one little notion I want to express,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] simply that I may not forget it.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Very good, tell me your little notion,” Raskolnikov stood waiting, pale
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and grave before him.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] playful, psychological idea…. When you were writing your article, surely
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] you couldn’t have helped, he-he! fancying yourself… just a little, an
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] ‘extraordinary’ man, uttering a new word in your sense…. That’s so, isn’t
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] it?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Quite possibly,” Raskolnikov answered contemptuously.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Razumihin made a movement.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “And, if so, could you bring yourself in case of worldly difficulties and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] hardship or for some service to humanity—to overstep obstacles?… For
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] instance, to rob and murder?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] And again he winked with his left eye, and laughed noiselessly just as
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] before.

[PAGE 263]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “If I did I certainly should not tell you,” Raskolnikov answered with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] defiant and haughty contempt.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Foo! how obvious and insolent that is!” Raskolnikov thought with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] repulsion.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Allow me to observe,” he answered dryly, “that I don’t consider myself
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] a Mahomet or a Napoleon, nor any personage of that kind, and not being
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] one of them I cannot tell you how I should act.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Oh, come, don’t we all think ourselves Napoleons now in Russia?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Porfiry Petrovitch said with alarming familiarity.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Perhaps it was one of these future Napoleons who did for Alyona

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov did not speak, but looked firmly and intently at Porfiry.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Razumihin was scowling gloomily. He seemed before this to be noticing
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] something. He looked angrily around. There was a minute of gloomy

[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=233 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter Six

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] had seen it. Who would own it against himself?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “If I had done that thing, I should certainly have said that I had seen the

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “But why speak against yourself?”

[PAGE 267]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Because only peasants, or the most inexperienced novices deny

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] happiness of all and so my heart is at peace. Ha-ha! Why have you let me

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] more nor less (so the rest would have gone to a monastery, according to her

[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=213 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter Three
[L s=27.5 r=1.83 x=77 y=170 b=0 i=0 f=f3] T
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=347 y=179 b=0 i=0 f=f3] he had never expected such an
[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=94 y=183 b=0 i=0 f=f3] HE FACT WAS THAT UP TO THE LAST MOMENT
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] ending; he had been overbearing to the last degree, never dreaming that two
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=219 b=0 i=0 f=f3] destitute and defenceless women could escape from his control. This
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f3] conviction was strengthened by his vanity and conceit, a conceit to the point
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=255 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of fatuity. Pyotr Petrovitch, who had made his way up from insignificance,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was morbidly given to self-admiration, had the highest opinion of his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] intelligence and capacities, and sometimes even gloated in solitude over his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] image in the glass. But what he loved and valued above all was the money
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f3] he had amassed by his labour, and by all sorts of devices: that money made
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] him the equal of all who had been his superiors.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=363 b=0 i=0 f=f3] When he had bitterly reminded Dounia that he had decided to take her

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=417 b=0 i=0 f=f3] yet, when he made Dounia his offer, he was fully aware of the

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=453 b=0 i=0 f=f3] contradicted by Marfa Petrovna, and was by then disbelieved by all the

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=561 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to admire it too. He had called on Raskolnikov with the feelings of a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=579 b=0 i=0 f=f3] benefactor who is about to reap the fruits of his good deeds and to hear

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=651 b=0 i=0 f=f3] For many years he had had voluptuous dreams of marriage, but he had gone

[PAGE 303]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] young, very pretty, of good birth and education, very timid, one who had

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov was waiting for him at the end of the passage.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov stopped once more.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] them. Do you understand me?”

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] lived. It was an old green house of three storeys. He found the porter and

[PAGE 319]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “What should I be without God?” she whispered rapidly, forcibly,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] glancing at him with suddenly flashing eyes, and squeezing his hand.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Ah, so that is it!” he thought.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “And what does God do for you?” he asked, probing her further.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Sonia was silent a long while, as though she could not answer. Her weak
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] chest kept heaving with emotion.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Be silent! Don’t ask! You don’t deserve!” she cried suddenly, looking
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] sternly and wrathfully at him.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “That’s it, that’s it,” he repeated to himself.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “He does everything,” she whispered quickly, looking down again.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “That’s the way out! That’s the explanation,” he decided, scrutinising
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] her with eager curiosity, with a new, strange, almost morbid feeling. He

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] old and worn.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Where did you get that?” he called to her across the room.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] at him.

[PAGE 320]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “You are not looking in the right place…. It’s in the fourth gospel,” she

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “In three weeks’ time they’ll welcome me in the madhouse! I shall be
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] there if I am not in a worse place,” he muttered to himself.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Sonia heard Raskolnikov’s request distrustfully and moved hesitatingly

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] degree of fury it might drive him, feeling that that fury might drive him
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] mad.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] criminal, or were somehow mixed up in this damnable business, would you
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] insist that you were not delirious but in full possession of your faculties?
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] And so emphatically and persistently? Would it be possible? Quite

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] There was a note of slyness in this inquiry. Raskolnikov drew back on

[PAGE 339]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was not weighing his words as he ought. “You want to frighten me… or you

[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=233 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter Six

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] For an instant there was no answer, but it was evident that there were

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=363 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “He is not wanted! Take him away! Let him wait! What’s he doing
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=381 b=0 i=0 f=f3] here? How irregular!” cried Porfiry, rushing to the door.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=453 b=0 i=0 f=f3] This man’s appearance was at first sight very strange. He stared straight

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=633 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Go away, it’s too soon! Wait till you are sent for!… Why have you
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=651 b=0 i=0 f=f3] brought him so soon?” Porfiry Petrovitch muttered, extremely annoyed, and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=669 b=0 i=0 f=f3] as it were thrown out of his reckoning.

[PAGE 344]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “What’s the matter?” cried Porfiry, surprised.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I am guilty! Mine is the sin! I am the murderer,” Nikolay articulated
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] suddenly, rather breathless, but speaking fairly loudly.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] For ten seconds there was silence as though all had been struck dumb;
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] even the warder stepped back, mechanically retreated to the door, and stood

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Ach! What did you kill them with?”

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Did you do it alone?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Yes, alone. And Mitka is not guilty and had no share in it.”

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] But how imminent? His position gradually became clear to him.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] some answer….

[PAGE 349]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] So this was the solution of yesterday’s horror. The most awful thought

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “To-day?”

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and he went out more confident than ever.

[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=94 y=183 b=0 i=0 f=f3] HE MORNING THAT FOLLOWED THE FATEFUL INTERVIEW
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] mother brought sobering influences to bear on Pyotr Petrovitch. Intensely
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=219 b=0 i=0 f=f3] unpleasant as it was, he was forced little by little to accept as a fact beyond
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f3] recall what had seemed to him only the day before fantastic and incredible.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=255 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The black snake of wounded vanity had been gnawing at his heart all night.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] When he got out of bed, Pyotr Petrovitch immediately looked in the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] looking-glass. He was afraid that he had jaundice. However his health
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] seemed unimpaired so far, and looking at his noble, clear-skinned
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f3] countenance which had grown fattish of late, Pyotr Petrovitch for an instant
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was positively comforted in the conviction that he would find another bride
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=363 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and, perhaps, even a better one. But coming back to the sense of his present
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=381 b=0 i=0 f=f3] position, he turned aside and spat vigorously, which excited a sarcastic
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=399 b=0 i=0 f=f3] smile in Andrey Semyonovitch Lebeziatnikov, the young friend with whom
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=417 b=0 i=0 f=f3] he was staying. That smile Pyotr Petrovitch noticed, and at once set it down
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=435 b=0 i=0 f=f3] against his young friend’s account. He had set down a good many points
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=453 b=0 i=0 f=f3] against him of late. His anger was redoubled when he reflected that he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=471 b=0 i=0 f=f3] ought not to have told Andrey Semyonovitch about the result of yesterday’s
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=489 b=0 i=0 f=f3] interview. That was the second mistake he had made in temper, through
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=507 b=0 i=0 f=f3] impulsiveness and irritability…. Moreover, all that morning one
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=525 b=0 i=0 f=f3] unpleasantness followed another. He even found a hitch awaiting him in his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=543 b=0 i=0 f=f3] legal case in the senate. He was particularly irritated by the owner of the flat
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=561 b=0 i=0 f=f3] which had been taken in view of his approaching marriage and was being
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=579 b=0 i=0 f=f3] redecorated at his own expense; the owner, a rich German tradesman, would
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=597 b=0 i=0 f=f3] not entertain the idea of breaking the contract which had just been signed
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=615 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and insisted on the full forfeit money, though Pyotr Petrovitch would be
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=633 b=0 i=0 f=f3] giving him back the flat practically redecorated. In the same way the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=651 b=0 i=0 f=f3] upholsterers refused to return a single rouble of the instalment paid for the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=669 b=0 i=0 f=f3] furniture purchased but not yet removed to the flat.

[PAGE 353]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Am I to get married simply for the sake of the furniture?” Pyotr

[PAGE 354]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Petrovitch and he went into his room, or rather Lebeziatnikov’s, somewhat

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Andrey Semyonovitch had been at home all the morning. The attitude of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Pyotr Petrovitch to this gentleman was strange, though perhaps natural.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Pyotr Petrovitch had despised and hated him from the day he came to stay
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] with him and at the same time he seemed somewhat afraid of him. He had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] not come to stay with him on his arrival in Petersburg simply from

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] uneasiness at the thought of transferring his business to Petersburg. He was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] afraid of this as little children are sometimes panic-stricken. Some years
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] before, when he was just entering on his own career, he had come upon two

[PAGE 355]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to fear from them? Would they expose any enterprise of his? And what
