[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] questions presented themselves.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Andrey Semyonovitch was an anæmic, scrofulous little man, with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] strangely flaxen mutton-chop whiskers of which he was very proud. He was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] a clerk and had almost always something wrong with his eyes. He was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] rather soft-hearted, but self-confident and sometimes extremely conceited in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] speech, which had an absurd effect, incongruous with his little figure. He

[PAGE 366]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of first importance, I agree; but the question of children has another

[PAGE 367]
[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=221 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter Two
[L s=27.5 r=1.83 x=77 y=170 b=0 i=0 f=f3] I
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=292 y=179 b=0 i=0 f=f3] exactly what could have originated the
[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=86 y=183 b=0 i=0 f=f3] T WOULD BE DIFFICULT TO EXPLAIN
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] idea of that senseless dinner in Katerina Ivanovna’s disordered brain.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=219 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Nearly ten of the twenty roubles, given by Raskolnikov for Marmeladov’s
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f3] funeral, were wasted upon it. Possibly Katerina Ivanovna felt obliged to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=255 b=0 i=0 f=f3] honour the memory of the deceased “suitably,” that all the lodgers, and still
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] more Amalia Ivanovna, might know “that he was in no way their inferior,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and perhaps very much their superior,” and that no one had the right “to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] turn up his nose at him.” Perhaps the chief element was that peculiar “poor
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f3] man’s pride,” which compels many poor people to spend their last savings
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] on some traditional social ceremony, simply in order to do “like other
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=363 b=0 i=0 f=f3] people,” and not to “be looked down upon.” It is very probable, too, that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=381 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Katerina Ivanovna longed on this occasion, at the moment when she
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=399 b=0 i=0 f=f3] seemed to be abandoned by everyone, to show those “wretched
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=417 b=0 i=0 f=f3] contemptible lodgers” that she knew “how to do things, how to entertain”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=435 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and that she had been brought up “in a genteel, she might almost say
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=453 b=0 i=0 f=f3] aristocratic colonel’s family” and had not been meant for sweeping floors
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=471 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and washing the children’s rags at night. Even the poorest and most broken-
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=489 b=0 i=0 f=f3] spirited people are sometimes liable to these paroxysms of pride and vanity
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=507 b=0 i=0 f=f3] which take the form of an irresistible nervous craving. And Katerina
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=525 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Ivanovna was not broken-spirited; she might have been killed by
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=543 b=0 i=0 f=f3] circumstance, but her spirit could not have been broken, that is, she could
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=561 b=0 i=0 f=f3] not have been intimidated, her will could not be crushed. Moreover Sonia
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=579 b=0 i=0 f=f3] had said with good reason that her mind was unhinged. She could not be
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=597 b=0 i=0 f=f3] said to be insane, but for a year past she had been so harassed that her mind
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=615 b=0 i=0 f=f3] might well be overstrained. The later stages of consumption are apt, doctors
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=633 b=0 i=0 f=f3] tell us, to affect the intellect.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=651 b=0 i=0 f=f3] There was no great variety of wines, nor was there Madeira; but wine

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=687 b=0 i=0 f=f3] but in sufficient quantity. Besides the traditional rice and honey, there were

[PAGE 368]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] three or four dishes, one of which consisted of pancakes, all prepared in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Amalia Ivanovna’s kitchen. Two samovars were boiling, that tea and punch
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] might be offered after dinner. Katerina Ivanovna had herself seen to

[PAGE 379]
[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=213 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter Three
[L s=27.5 r=1.83 x=77 y=170 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “P

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] foolish woman understand that she can’t behave like this to a lady in

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=255 b=0 i=0 f=f3] hospitality protect these orphans.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Allow me, madam…. Allow me.” Pyotr Petrovitch waved her off.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Your papa as you are well aware I had not the honour of knowing”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] (someone laughed aloud) “and I do not intend to take part in your
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f3] everlasting squabbles with Amalia Ivanovna…. I have come here to speak
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of my own affairs… and I want to have a word with your stepdaughter,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=363 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Sofya… Ivanovna, I think it is? Allow me to pass.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=381 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Pyotr Petrovitch, edging by her, went to the opposite corner where
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=399 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Sonia was.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=417 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Katerina Ivanovna remained standing where she was, as though
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=435 b=0 i=0 f=f3] thunderstruck. She could not understand how Pyotr Petrovitch could deny
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=453 b=0 i=0 f=f3] having enjoyed her father’s hospitality. Though she had invented it herself,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=471 b=0 i=0 f=f3] she believed in it firmly by this time. She was struck too by the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=489 b=0 i=0 f=f3] businesslike, dry and even contemptuous menacing tone of Pyotr
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=507 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Petrovitch. All the clamour gradually died away at his entrance. Not only
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=525 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was this “serious business man” strikingly incongruous with the rest of the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=543 b=0 i=0 f=f3] party, but it was evident, too, that he had come upon some matter of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=561 b=0 i=0 f=f3] consequence, that some exceptional cause must have brought him and that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=579 b=0 i=0 f=f3] therefore something was going to happen. Raskolnikov, standing beside
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=597 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Sonia, moved aside to let him pass; Pyotr Petrovitch did not seem to notice
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=615 b=0 i=0 f=f3] him. A minute later Lebeziatnikov, too, appeared in the doorway; he did not

[PAGE 385]

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] afterwards. Lebeziatnikov strode into the room.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] them and he who dares most of all will be most in the right! So it has been

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Though Raskolnikov looked at Sonia as he said this, he no longer cared

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I divined then, Sonia,” he went on eagerly, “that power is only

[L s=27.5 r=1.00 x=257 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Part VI

[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=223 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter One
[L s=27.5 r=1.83 x=77 y=170 b=0 i=0 f=f3] A
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=272 y=179 b=0 i=0 f=f3] R : it was as though a fog had
[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=103 y=183 b=0 i=0 f=f3] STRANGE PERIOD BEGAN FOR ASKOLNIKOV
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] fallen upon him and wrapped him in a dreary solitude from which there was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=219 b=0 i=0 f=f3] no escape. Recalling that period long after, he believed that his mind had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f3] been clouded at times, and that it had continued so, with intervals, till the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=255 b=0 i=0 f=f3] final catastrophe. He was convinced that he had been mistaken about many
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] things at that time, for instance as to the date of certain events. Anyway,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] when he tried later on to piece his recollections together, he learnt a great
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] deal about himself from what other people told him. He had mixed up
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f3] incidents and had explained events as due to circumstances which existed
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] only in his imagination. At times he was a prey to agonies of morbid
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=363 b=0 i=0 f=f3] uneasiness, amounting sometimes to panic. But he remembered, too,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=381 b=0 i=0 f=f3] moments, hours, perhaps whole days, of complete apathy, which came upon
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=399 b=0 i=0 f=f3] him as a reaction from his previous terror and might be compared with the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=417 b=0 i=0 f=f3] abnormal insensibility, sometimes seen in the dying. He seemed to be trying
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=435 b=0 i=0 f=f3] in that latter stage to escape from a full and clear understanding of his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=453 b=0 i=0 f=f3] position. Certain essential facts which required immediate consideration
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=471 b=0 i=0 f=f3] were particularly irksome to him. How glad he would have been to be free
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=489 b=0 i=0 f=f3] from some cares, the neglect of which would have threatened him with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=507 b=0 i=0 f=f3] complete, inevitable ruin.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=525 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He was particularly worried about Svidrigaïlov, he might be said to be
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=543 b=0 i=0 f=f3] permanently thinking of Svidrigaïlov. From the time of Svidrigaïlov’s too
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=561 b=0 i=0 f=f3] menacing and unmistakable words in Sonia’s room at the moment of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=579 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Katerina Ivanovna’s death, the normal working of his mind seemed to break

[PAGE 425]

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “These last two days she hasn’t said a word to me, she hasn’t glanced at

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the incense rose in clouds; the priest read, “Give rest, oh Lord….”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov stayed all through the service. As he blessed them and took his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] leave, the priest looked round strangely. After the service, Raskolnikov

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] very miserable. If it had been possible to escape to some solitude, he would
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] have thought himself lucky, even if he had to spend his whole life there. But
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] although he had almost always been by himself of late, he had never been

[PAGE 436]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] journal, do you remember, on your first visit we talked of it? I jeered at you

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] had felt a great deal I recognised long before. I, too, have felt the same, so

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “It was not I murdered her,” Raskolnikov whispered like a frightened
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] child caught in the act.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] whispered sternly, with conviction.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Oh, stop that, what does that matter now? It would be a different

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] anything were to happen (though indeed I don’t believe in it and think you

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] hours with the notion of putting an end to the business in some other way,

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] note, only two lines, and mention the stone. It will be more generous.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Come, till we meet! Good thoughts and sound decisions to you!”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Porfiry went out, stooping and avoiding looking at Raskolnikov. The
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] latter went to the window and waited with irritable impatience till he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] calculated that Porfiry had reached the street and moved away. Then he too
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] went hurriedly out of the room.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f3] On the way, one question particularly worried him: had Svidrigaïlov
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=255 b=0 i=0 f=f3] been to Porfiry’s?

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] pondered again and again, went over Porfiry’s visit; no, he hadn’t been, of

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f3] But if he had not been yet, would he go? Meanwhile, for the present he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] fancied he couldn’t. Why? He could not have explained, but if he could, he

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] thing. Someone may be watching you besides me, and it won’t do you any
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] good. It’s nothing really to do with me and I can’t cure you, but, of course,

[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=220 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter Four

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] regret not having had more talk with you, but I shan’t lose sight of you….
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Only wait a bit.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Svidrigaïlov walked out of the restaurant. Raskolnikov walked out after
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] him. Svidrigaïlov was not however very drunk, the wine had affected him
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] for a moment, but it was passing off every minute. He was preoccupied

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] became very suspicious of Svidrigaïlov and resolved to follow him.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] They came out on to the pavement.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “You go to the right, and I to the left, or if you like, the other way. Only
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] adieu, mon plaisir, may we meet again.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] And he walked to the right towards the Hay Market.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=381 b=0 i=0 f=f3] should like to make certain myself.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=399 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov could hardly have said himself what he wanted and of

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the waiters and two little clerks. He was particularly drawn to these clerks
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] by the fact that they both had crooked noses, one bent to the left and the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] other to the right. They took him finally to a pleasure garden, where he paid
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f3] for their entrance. There was one lanky three-year-old pine-tree and three
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] bushes in the garden, besides a “Vauxhall,” which was in reality a drinking-

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=381 b=0 i=0 f=f3] standing round it. A chorus of wretched singers and a drunken but
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=399 b=0 i=0 f=f3] exceedingly depressed German clown from Munich with a red nose
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=417 b=0 i=0 f=f3] entertained the public. The clerks quarrelled with some other clerks and a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=435 b=0 i=0 f=f3] fight seemed imminent. Svidrigaïlov was chosen to decide the dispute. He
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=453 b=0 i=0 f=f3] listened to them for a quarter of an hour, but they shouted so loud that there
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=471 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was no possibility of understanding them. The only fact that seemed certain
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=489 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was that one of them had stolen something and had even succeeded in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=507 b=0 i=0 f=f3] selling it on the spot to a Jew, but would not share the spoil with his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=525 b=0 i=0 f=f3] companion. Finally it appeared that the stolen object was a teaspoon
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=543 b=0 i=0 f=f3] belonging to the Vauxhall. It was missed and the affair began to seem

[PAGE 481]

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was sleeping soundly, she had got warm under the blanket, and her pale
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] cheeks were flushed. But strange to say that flush seemed brighter and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] coarser than the rosy cheeks of childhood. “It’s a flush of fever,” thought
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Svidrigaïlov. It was like the flush from drinking, as though she had been
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] given a full glass to drink. Her crimson lips were hot and glowing; but what
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was this? He suddenly fancied that her long black eyelashes were quivering,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] as though the lids were opening and a sly crafty eye peeped out with an
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] unchildlike wink, as though the little girl were not asleep, but pretending.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Yes, it was so. Her lips parted in a smile. The corners of her mouth
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] quivered, as though she were trying to control them. But now she quite gave
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] up all effort, now it was a grin, a broad grin; there was something
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] shameless, provocative in that quite unchildish face; it was depravity, it was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the face of a harlot, the shameless face of a French harlot. Now both eyes
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] opened wide; they turned a glowing, shameless glance upon him; they
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] laughed, invited him…. There was something infinitely hideous and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] shocking in that laugh, in those eyes, in such nastiness in the face of a child.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “What, at five years old?” Svidrigaïlov muttered in genuine horror. “What
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] does it mean?” And now she turned to him, her little face all aglow, holding
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] out her arms…. “Accursed child!” Svidrigaïlov cried, raising his hand to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] strike her, but at that moment he woke up.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He was in the same bed, still wrapped in the blanket. The candle had not
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] been lighted, and daylight was streaming in at the windows.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I’ve had nightmare all night!” He got up angrily, feeling utterly
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] shattered; his bones ached. There was a thick mist outside and he could see
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] nothing. It was nearly five. He had overslept himself! He got up, put on his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] still damp jacket and overcoat. Feeling the revolver in his pocket, he took it
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] out and then he sat down, took a notebook out of his pocket and in the most
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] conspicuous place on the title page wrote a few lines in large letters.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Reading them over, he sank into thought with his elbows on the table. The

[PAGE 491]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] revolver and the notebook lay beside him. Some flies woke up and settled
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] on the untouched veal, which was still on the table. He stared at them and at
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] last with his free right hand began trying to catch one. He tried till he was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] tired, but could not catch it. At last, realising that he was engaged in this
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] interesting pursuit, he started, got up and walked resolutely out of the room.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] A minute later he was in the street.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] A thick milky mist hung over the town. Svidrigaïlov walked along the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] slippery dirty wooden pavement towards the Little Neva. He was picturing
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the waters of the Little Neva swollen in the night, Petrovsky Island, the wet
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] paths, the wet grass, the wet trees and bushes and at last the bush…. He
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] began ill-humouredly staring at the houses, trying to think of something
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] else. There was not a cabman or a passer-by in the street. The bright yellow,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] wooden, little houses looked dirty and dejected with their closed shutters.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The cold and damp penetrated his whole body and he began to shiver. From
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] time to time he came across shop signs and read each carefully. At last he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] reached the end of the wooden pavement and came to a big stone house. A
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] dirty, shivering dog crossed his path with its tail between its legs. A man in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] a greatcoat lay face downwards; dead drunk, across the pavement. He
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] looked at him and went on. A high tower stood up on the left. “Bah!” he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] shouted, “here is a place. Why should it be Petrovsky? It will be in the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] presence of an official witness anyway….”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He almost smiled at this new thought and turned into the street where
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] there was the big house with the tower. At the great closed gates of the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] house, a little man stood with his shoulder leaning against them, wrapped in

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “This isn’t the place.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I am going to foreign parts, brother.”

[PAGE 492]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “To foreign parts?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “To America.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “America.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Svidrigaïlov took out the revolver and cocked it. Achilles raised his

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Why shouldn’t it be the place?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Because it isn’t.”

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He put the revolver to his right temple.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “You can’t do it here, it’s not the place,” cried Achilles, rousing himself,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] his eyes growing bigger and bigger.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Svidrigaïlov pulled the trigger.

[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=211 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter Seven
[L s=27.5 r=1.83 x=77 y=170 b=0 i=0 f=f3] T
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=167 y=179 b=0 i=0 f=f3] , about seven o’clock in the evening, Raskolnikov was on his
[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=94 y=183 b=0 i=0 f=f3] HE SAME DAY
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] way to his mother’s and sister’s lodging—the lodging in Bakaleyev’s house
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=219 b=0 i=0 f=f3] which Razumihin had found for them. The stairs went up from the street.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov walked with lagging steps, as though still hesitating whether to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=255 b=0 i=0 f=f3] go or not. But nothing would have turned him back: his decision was taken.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Besides, it doesn’t matter, they still know nothing,” he thought, “and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] they are used to thinking of me as eccentric.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He was appallingly dressed: his clothes torn and dirty, soaked with a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f3] night’s rain. His face was almost distorted from fatigue, exposure, the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] inward conflict that had lasted for twenty-four hours. He had spent all the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=363 b=0 i=0 f=f3] previous night alone, God knows where. But anyway he had reached a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=381 b=0 i=0 f=f3] decision.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=399 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He knocked at the door which was opened by his mother. Dounia was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=417 b=0 i=0 f=f3] not at home. Even the servant happened to be out. At first Pulcheria
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=435 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Alexandrovna was speechless with joy and surprise; then she took him by
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=453 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the hand and drew him into the room.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=471 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Here you are!” she began, faltering with joy. “Don’t be angry with me,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=489 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Rodya, for welcoming you so foolishly with tears: I am laughing not crying.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=507 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Did you think I was crying? No, I am delighted, but I’ve got into such a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=525 b=0 i=0 f=f3] stupid habit of shedding tears. I’ve been like that ever since your father’s
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=543 b=0 i=0 f=f3] death. I cry for anything. Sit down, dear boy, you must be tired; I see you
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=561 b=0 i=0 f=f3] are. Ah, how muddy you are.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=579 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I was in the rain yesterday, mother….” Raskolnikov began.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=597 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “No, no,” Pulcheria Alexandrovna hurriedly interrupted, “you thought I
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=615 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was going to cross-question you in the womanish way I used to; don’t be
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=633 b=0 i=0 f=f3] anxious, I understand, I understand it all: now I’ve learned the ways here
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=651 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and truly I see for myself that they are better. I’ve made up my mind once
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=669 b=0 i=0 f=f3] for all: how could I understand your plans and expect you to give an

[PAGE 494]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] what ideas you are hatching; so it’s not for me to keep nudging your elbow,

[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=216 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter Eight

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=218 y=179 b=0 i=0 f=f3] S ’ , it was already getting dark. All day
[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=103 y=183 b=0 i=0 f=f3] HEN HE WENT INTO ONIA S ROOM

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f3] words that Sonia knew. We will not describe the conversation and tears of

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=435 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Dounia at last became impatient and, leaving Sonia, went to her

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=615 b=0 i=0 f=f3] make him live?” she thought at last in despair.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Ilya Petrovitch held out his hand.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I only wanted… I came to see Zametov.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I understand, I understand, and it’s a pleasure to see you.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I… am very glad… good-bye,” Raskolnikov smiled.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He went out; he reeled, he was overtaken with giddiness and did not
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] know what he was doing. He began going down the stairs, supporting
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] himself with his right hand against the wall. He fancied that a porter pushed
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] past him on his way upstairs to the police office, that a dog in the lower
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] storey kept up a shrill barking and that a woman flung a rolling-pin at it and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] shouted. He went down and out into the yard. There, not far from the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] entrance, stood Sonia, pale and horror-stricken. She looked wildly at him.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He stood still before her. There was a look of poignant agony, of despair, in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] her face. She clasped her hands. His lips worked in an ugly, meaningless
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] smile. He stood still a minute, grinned and went back to the police office.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Ilya Petrovitch had sat down and was rummaging among some papers.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Before him stood the same peasant who had pushed by on the stairs.

[PAGE 511]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Hulloa! Back again! have you left something behind? What’s the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] matter?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov, with white lips and staring eyes, came slowly nearer. He
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] walked right to the table, leaned his hand on it, tried to say something, but
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] could not; only incoherent sounds were audible.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “You are feeling ill, a chair! Here, sit down! Some water!”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov dropped on to a chair, but he kept his eyes fixed on the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] face of Ilya Petrovitch, which expressed unpleasant surprise. Both looked at

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Drink some water.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov refused the water with his hand, and softly and brokenly,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] but distinctly said:
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=308 b=0 i=1 f=f5] “It was I killed the old pawnbroker woman and her sister Lizaveta with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=1 f=f5] an axe and robbed them.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Ilya Petrovitch opened his mouth. People ran up on all sides.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov repeated his statement.
[L s=11.3 r=0.75 x=77 y=388 b=0 i=0 f=f3] * Dostoevsky appears to have forgotten that it is after sunset, and that the last time Raskolnikov
[L s=11.3 r=0.75 x=94 y=406 b=0 i=0 f=f3] visited the police office at two in the afternoon he was reproached for coming too late. —

[PAGE 512]
[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=249 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Epilogue
[L s=16.9 r=1.12 x=304 y=162 b=0 i=0 f=f1] I
[L s=27.5 r=1.83 x=77 y=209 b=0 i=0 f=f3] S
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=127 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] . O banks of a broad solitary river stands a town, one of the
[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=92 y=222 b=0 i=0 f=f3] IBERIA N THE
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=240 b=0 i=0 f=f3] administrative centres of Russia; in the town there is a fortress, in the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=258 b=0 i=0 f=f3] fortress there is a prison. In the prison the second-class convict Rodion
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=276 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov has been confined for nine months. Almost a year and a half
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=294 b=0 i=0 f=f3] has passed since his crime.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=312 b=0 i=0 f=f3] There had been little difficulty about his trial. The criminal adhered
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=330 b=0 i=0 f=f3] exactly, firmly, and clearly to his statement. He did not confuse nor
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=348 b=0 i=0 f=f3] misrepresent the facts, nor soften them in his own interest, nor omit the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=366 b=0 i=0 f=f3] smallest detail. He explained every incident of the murder, the secret of the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=384 b=0 i=0 f=f3] pledge (the piece of wood with a strip of metal) which was found in the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=402 b=0 i=0 f=f3] murdered woman’s hand. He described minutely how he had taken her
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=420 b=0 i=0 f=f3] keys, what they were like, as well as the chest and its contents; he explained
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=438 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the mystery of Lizaveta’s murder; described how Koch and, after him, the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=456 b=0 i=0 f=f3] student knocked, and repeated all they had said to one another; how he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=474 b=0 i=0 f=f3] afterwards had run downstairs and heard Nikolay and Dmitri shouting; how
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=492 b=0 i=0 f=f3] he had hidden in the empty flat and afterwards gone home. He ended by
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=510 b=0 i=0 f=f3] indicating the stone in the yard off the Voznesensky Prospect under which
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=528 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the purse and the trinkets were found. The whole thing, in fact, was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=546 b=0 i=0 f=f3] perfectly clear. The lawyers and the judges were very much struck, among
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=564 b=0 i=0 f=f3] other things, by the fact that he had hidden the trinkets and the purse under
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=582 b=0 i=0 f=f3] a stone, without making use of them, and that, what was more, he did not
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=600 b=0 i=0 f=f3] now remember what the trinkets were like, or even how many there were.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=618 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The fact that he had never opened the purse and did not even know how
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=636 b=0 i=0 f=f3] much was in it seemed incredible. There turned out to be in the purse three
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=654 b=0 i=0 f=f3] hundred and seventeen roubles and sixty copecks. From being so long
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=672 b=0 i=0 f=f3] under the stone, some of the most valuable notes lying uppermost had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=690 b=0 i=0 f=f3] suffered from the damp. They were a long while trying to discover why the

[PAGE 513]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] accused man should tell a lie about this, when about everything else he had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] made a truthful and straightforward confession. Finally some of the lawyers
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] more versed in psychology admitted that it was possible he had really not
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] looked into the purse, and so didn’t know what was in it when he hid it
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] under the stone. But they immediately drew the deduction that the crime
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] could only have been committed through temporary mental derangement,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] through homicidal mania, without object or the pursuit of gain. This fell in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] with the most recent fashionable theory of temporary insanity, so often
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] applied in our days in criminal cases. Moreover Raskolnikov’s
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] hypochondriacal condition was proved by many witnesses, by Dr.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Zossimov, his former fellow students, his landlady and her servant. All this
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] pointed strongly to the conclusion that Raskolnikov was not quite like an
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] ordinary murderer and robber, but that there was another element in the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] case.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] To the intense annoyance of those who maintained this opinion, the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] criminal scarcely attempted to defend himself. To the decisive question as
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to what motive impelled him to the murder and the robbery, he answered
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] very clearly with the coarsest frankness that the cause was his miserable
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] position, his poverty and helplessness, and his desire to provide for his first
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] steps in life by the help of the three thousand roubles he had reckoned on
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] finding. He had been led to the murder through his shallow and cowardly
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] nature, exasperated moreover by privation and failure. To the question what
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] led him to confess, he answered that it was his heartfelt repentance. All this
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was almost coarse….
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The sentence however was more merciful than could have been
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] expected, perhaps partly because the criminal had not tried to justify
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] himself, but had rather shown a desire to exaggerate his guilt. All the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] strange and peculiar circumstances of the crime were taken into
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] consideration. There could be no doubt of the abnormal and poverty-
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] stricken condition of the criminal at the time. The fact that he had made no
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] use of what he had stolen was put down partly to the effect of remorse,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] partly to his abnormal mental condition at the time of the crime.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Incidentally the murder of Lizaveta served indeed to confirm the last
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] hypothesis: a man commits two murders and forgets that the door is open!
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Finally, the confession, at the very moment when the case was hopelessly
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] muddled by the false evidence given by Nikolay through melancholy and

[PAGE 514]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] fanaticism, and when, moreover, there were no proofs against the real

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] discovered and proved that while Raskolnikov was at the university he had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] helped a poor consumptive fellow student and had spent his last penny on
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] supporting him for six months, and when this student died, leaving a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] decrepit old father whom he had maintained almost from his thirteenth year,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov had got the old man into a hospital and paid for his funeral
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] when he died. Raskolnikov’s landlady bore witness, too, that when they had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] lived in another house at Five Corners, Raskolnikov had rescued two little

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] investigated and fairly well confirmed by many witnesses. These facts made

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] And in the end the criminal was, in consideration of extenuating
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] circumstances, condemned to penal servitude in the second class for a term

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] At the very beginning of the trial Raskolnikov’s mother fell ill. Dounia
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and Razumihin found it possible to get her out of Petersburg during the

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to be able to follow every step of the trial and at the same time to see
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Avdotya Romanovna as often as possible. Pulcheria Alexandrovna’s illness
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was a strange nervous one and was accompanied by a partial derangement
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of her intellect.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] When Dounia returned from her last interview with her brother, she had

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] commission, which would bring him in the end money and reputation.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] But they were struck by the fact that Pulcheria Alexandrovna never

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] They began to be frightened at last at Pulcheria Alexandrovna’s strange

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Five months after Raskolnikov’s confession, he was sentenced.

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] secure livelihood during the next three or four years, and saving up a certain
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] sum, to emigrate to Siberia, a country rich in every natural resource and in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] need of workers, active men and capital. There they would settle in the

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] wept at parting.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov had been very dreamy for a few days before. He asked a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] great deal about his mother and was constantly anxious about her. He
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] worried so much about her that it alarmed Dounia. When he heard about his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] mother’s illness he became very gloomy. With Sonia he was particularly
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] reserved all the time. With the help of the money left to her by Svidrigaïlov,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Sonia had long ago made her preparations to follow the party of convicts in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] which he was despatched to Siberia. Not a word passed between

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] out of prison. He predicted that their mother’s illness would soon have a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] fatal ending. Sonia and he at last set off.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Two months later Dounia was married to Razumihin. It was a quiet and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] sorrowful wedding; Porfiry Petrovitch and Zossimov were invited however.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] During all this period Razumihin wore an air of resolute determination.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Dounia put implicit faith in his carrying out his plans and indeed she could
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] not but believe in him. He displayed a rare strength of will. Among other
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] things he began attending university lectures again in order to take his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] degree. They were continually making plans for the future; both counted on
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] settling in Siberia within five years at least. Till then they rested their hopes
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] on Sonia.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Pulcheria Alexandrovna was delighted to give her blessing to Dounia’s
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] marriage with Razumihin; but after the marriage she became even more
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] melancholy and anxious. To give her pleasure Razumihin told her how
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov had looked after the poor student and his decrepit father and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] how a year ago he had been burnt and injured in rescuing two little children
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] from a fire. These two pieces of news excited Pulcheria Alexandrovna’s
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] disordered imagination almost to ecstasy. She was continually talking about
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] them, even entering into conversation with strangers in the street, though

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] wherever she could capture a listener, she would begin the discourse about

[PAGE 517]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] her son, his article, how he had helped the student, how he had been burnt
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] at the fire, and so on! Dounia did not know how to restrain her. Apart from
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the danger of her morbid excitement, there was the risk of someone’s
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] recalling Raskolnikov’s name and speaking of the recent trial. Pulcheria
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Alexandrovna found out the address of the mother of the two children her
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] son had saved and insisted on going to see her.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] At last her restlessness reached an extreme point. She would sometimes
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] begin to cry suddenly and was often ill and feverishly delirious. One

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] began to do up her room for him, to clean the furniture, to wash and put up
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] new hangings and so on. Dounia was anxious, but said nothing and helped
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] her to arrange the room. After a fatiguing day spent in continual fancies, in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] joyful day-dreams and tears, Pulcheria Alexandrovna was taken ill in the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] night and by morning she was feverish and delirious. It was brain fever. She
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] died within a fortnight. In her delirium she dropped words which showed
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that she knew a great deal more about her son’s terrible fate than they had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] supposed.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] For a long time Raskolnikov did not know of his mother’s death, though
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] a regular correspondence had been maintained from the time he reached
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Siberia. It was carried on by means of Sonia, who wrote every month to the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Razumihins and received an answer with unfailing regularity. At first they
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] found Sonia’s letters dry and unsatisfactory, but later on they came to the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] conclusion that the letters could not be better, for from these letters they

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] letters were full of the most matter-of-fact detail, the simplest and clearest

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] precision. There could be no mistake, because nothing was given but facts.

[PAGE 518]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] But Dounia and her husband could get little comfort out of the news,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] especially at first. Sonia wrote that he was constantly sullen and not ready

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] anything else, declaring that all this fuss about him only annoyed him.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Sonia wrote further that in prison he shared the same room with the rest,

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] were crowded, miserable and unhealthy; that he slept on a plank bed with a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] rug under him and was unwilling to make any other arrangement. But that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] he lived so poorly and roughly, not from any plan or design, but simply

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] many houses. But she did not mention that the authorities were, through

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] At last the news came (Dounia had indeed noticed signs of alarm and

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] his fellow prisoners did not like him, that he kept silent for days at a time

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] taken very seriously ill and was in the convict ward of the hospital.

[L s=27.5 r=1.83 x=77 y=256 b=0 i=0 f=f3] H
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=158 y=266 b=0 i=0 f=f3] a long time. But it was not the horrors of prison life, not the
[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=97 y=269 b=0 i=0 f=f3] E WAS ILL
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=287 b=0 i=0 f=f3] hard labour, the bad food, the shaven head, or the patched clothes that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=305 b=0 i=0 f=f3] crushed him. What did he care for all those trials and hardships! he was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=323 b=0 i=0 f=f3] even glad of the hard work. Physically exhausted, he could at least reckon
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=341 b=0 i=0 f=f3] on a few hours of quiet sleep. And what was the food to him—the thin
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=359 b=0 i=0 f=f3] cabbage soup with beetles floating in it? In the past as a student he had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=377 b=0 i=0 f=f3] often not had even that. His clothes were warm and suited to his manner of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=395 b=0 i=0 f=f3] life. He did not even feel the fetters. Was he ashamed of his shaven head
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=413 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and parti-coloured coat? Before whom? Before Sonia? Sonia was afraid of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=431 b=0 i=0 f=f3] him, how could he be ashamed before her? And yet he was ashamed even
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=449 b=0 i=0 f=f3] before Sonia, whom he tortured because of it with his contemptuous rough
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=467 b=0 i=0 f=f3] manner. But it was not his shaven head and his fetters he was ashamed of:
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=485 b=0 i=0 f=f3] his pride had been stung to the quick. It was wounded pride that made him
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=503 b=0 i=0 f=f3] ill. Oh, how happy he would have been if he could have blamed himself!
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=521 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He could have borne anything then, even shame and disgrace. But he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=539 b=0 i=0 f=f3] judged himself severely, and his exasperated conscience found no
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=557 b=0 i=0 f=f3] particularly terrible fault in his past, except a simple blunder which might
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=575 b=0 i=0 f=f3] happen to anyone. He was ashamed just because he, Raskolnikov, had so
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=593 b=0 i=0 f=f3] hopelessly, stupidly come to grief through some decree of blind fate, and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=611 b=0 i=0 f=f3] must humble himself and submit to “the idiocy” of a sentence, if he were
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=629 b=0 i=0 f=f3] anyhow to be at peace.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=647 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Vague and objectless anxiety in the present, and in the future a continual
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=665 b=0 i=0 f=f3] sacrifice leading to nothing—that was all that lay before him. And what
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=683 b=0 i=0 f=f3] comfort was it to him that at the end of eight years he would only be thirty-
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=701 b=0 i=0 f=f3] two and able to begin a new life! What had he to live for? What had he to

[PAGE 520]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] look forward to? Why should he strive? To live in order to exist? Why, he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] had been ready a thousand times before to give up existence for the sake of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] an idea, for a hope, even for a fancy. Mere existence had always been too
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] little for him; he had always wanted more. Perhaps it was just because of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the strength of his desires that he had thought himself a man to whom more
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was permissible than to others.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] And if only fate would have sent him repentance—burning repentance
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that would have torn his heart and robbed him of sleep, that repentance, the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] awful agony of which brings visions of hanging or drowning! Oh, he would
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] have been glad of it! Tears and agonies would at least have been life. But he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] did not repent of his crime.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] At least he might have found relief in raging at his stupidity, as he had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] raged at the grotesque blunders that had brought him to prison. But now in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] prison, in freedom, he thought over and criticised all his actions again and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] by no means found them so blundering and so grotesque as they had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] seemed at the fatal time.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “In what way,” he asked himself, “was my theory stupider than others
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that have swarmed and clashed from the beginning of the world? One has
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] only to look at the thing quite independently, broadly, and uninfluenced by
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] commonplace ideas, and my idea will by no means seem so… strange. Oh,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] sceptics and halfpenny philosophers, why do you halt half-way!
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Why does my action strike them as so horrible?” he said to himself. “Is
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] it because it was a crime? What is meant by crime? My conscience is at
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] rest. Of course, it was a legal crime, of course, the letter of the law was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] broken and blood was shed. Well, punish me for the letter of the law… and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that’s enough. Of course, in that case many of the benefactors of mankind
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] who snatched power for themselves instead of inheriting it ought to have
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] been punished at their first steps. But those men succeeded and so they were
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] right, and I didn’t, and so I had no right to have taken that step.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] It was only in that that he recognised his criminality, only in the fact that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] he had been unsuccessful and had confessed it.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He suffered too from the question: why had he not killed himself? Why
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] had he stood looking at the river and preferred to confess? Was the desire to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] live so strong and was it so hard to overcome it? Had not Svidrigaïlov
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] overcome it, although he was afraid of death?

[PAGE 521]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] In misery he asked himself this question, and could not understand that,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] at the very time he had been standing looking into the river, he had perhaps
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] been dimly conscious of the fundamental falsity in himself and his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] convictions. He didn’t understand that that consciousness might be the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] promise of a future crisis, of a new view of life and of his future
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] resurrection.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He preferred to attribute it to the dead weight of instinct which he could
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] not step over, again through weakness and meanness. He looked at his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] fellow prisoners and was amazed to see how they all loved life and prized
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] it. It seemed to him that they loved and valued life more in prison than in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] freedom. What terrible agonies and privations some of them, the tramps for
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] instance, had endured! Could they care so much for a ray of sunshine, for
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the primeval forest, the cold spring hidden away in some unseen spot,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] which the tramp had marked three years before, and longed to see again, as
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] he might to see his sweetheart, dreaming of the green grass round it and the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] bird singing in the bush? As he went on he saw still more inexplicable
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] examples.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] In prison, of course, there was a great deal he did not see and did not
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] want to see; he lived as it were with downcast eyes. It was loathsome and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] unbearable for him to look. But in the end there was much that surprised
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] him and he began, as it were involuntarily, to notice much that he had not
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] suspected before. What surprised him most of all was the terrible
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] impossible gulf that lay between him and all the rest. They seemed to be a

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] hostility. He felt and knew the reasons of his isolation, but he would never
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] have admitted till then that those reasons were so deep and strong. There
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] were some Polish exiles, political prisoners, among them. They simply

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] look upon them like that. He saw that these ignorant men were in many

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] as contemptuous, a former officer and two seminarists. Raskolnikov saw
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] their mistake as clearly. He was disliked and avoided by everyone; they

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “You’re a gentleman,” they used to say. “You shouldn’t hack about with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] an axe; that’s not a gentleman’s work.”

[PAGE 522]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The second week in Lent, his turn came to take the sacrament with his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] gang. He went to church and prayed with the others. A quarrel broke out
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] one day, he did not know how. All fell on him at once in a fury.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “You’re an infidel! You don’t believe in God,” they shouted. “You ought
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to be killed.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He had never talked to them about God nor his belief, but they wanted
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to kill him as an infidel. He said nothing. One of the prisoners rushed at him
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] in a perfect frenzy. Raskolnikov awaited him calmly and silently; his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] eyebrows did not quiver, his face did not flinch. The guard succeeded in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] intervening between him and his assailant, or there would have been
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] bloodshed.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] There was another question he could not decide: why were they all so

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He was in the hospital from the middle of Lent till after Easter. When he

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] furious. But never had men considered themselves so intellectual and so

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] did not know how to judge and could not agree what to consider evil and

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] agonies of the past! Everything, even his crime, his sentence and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] imprisonment, seemed to him now in the first rush of feeling an external,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] strange fact with which he had no concern. But he could not think for long
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] together of anything that evening, and he could not have analysed anything
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] consciously; he was simply feeling. Life had stepped into the place of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] theory and something quite different would work itself out in his mind.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Under his pillow lay the New Testament. He took it up mechanically.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The book belonged to Sonia; it was the one from which she had read the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] raising of Lazarus to him. At first he was afraid that she would worry him

[PAGE 526]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] about religion, would talk about the gospel and pester him with books. But
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to his great surprise she had not once approached the subject and had not
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] even offered him the Testament. He had asked her for it himself not long
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] before his illness and she brought him the book without a word. Till now he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] had not opened it.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He did not open it now, but one thought passed through his mind: “Can
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] her convictions not be mine now? Her feelings, her aspirations at least….”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] She too had been greatly agitated that day, and at night she was taken ill
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] again. But she was so happy—and so unexpectedly happy—that she was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] almost frightened of her happiness. Seven years, only seven years! At the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] beginning of their happiness at some moments they were both ready to look
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] on those seven years as though they were seven days. He did not know that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the new life would not be given him for nothing, that he would have to pay
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] dearly for it, that it would cost him great striving, great suffering.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] But that is the beginning of a new story—the story of the gradual
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] renewal of a man, the story of his gradual regeneration, of his passing from
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] one world into another, of his initiation into a new unknown life. That might
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] be the subject of a new story, but our present story is ended.

[L s=16.9 r=1.12 x=244 y=114 b=1 i=0 f=f7] About BB eBooks
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f8] BB eBooks is a Bangkok-based company that was founded in 2013 to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=219 b=0 i=0 f=f8] provide fast, reliable, and professional formatting and design services for
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f8] books created by independent authors and small presses. All books
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=255 b=0 i=0 f=f8] created by BB eBooks are immediately ready for sale at all major online
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f8] retailers (including Amazon, iTunes, Kobo, and many others). BB eBooks
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f8] acts as a business-to-business contractor for the worldwide publishing
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f8] community to bring about better standards for book layout and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f8] production. BB eBooks is also committed to ethical conduct and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f8] promoting a sexual harassment-free work environment.
